Description: Stop using HSL. Use OKLCH (or LCH) instead. It's perceptually uniform, meaning equal steps in lightness look equal—unlike HSL where 50% lightness in yellow looks bright while 50% in blue looks dark.
As you move toward white or black, reduce chroma (saturation). High chroma at extreme lightness looks garish.
Don't: Use high chroma at extreme lightness

Description: Pure gray is dead. Add a subtle hint of your brand hue to all neutrals. The chroma is tiny (0.01) but perceptible.
Do: Add subtle brand hue to neutrals

Description: A complete system needs: Primary (1 color, 3-5 shades), Neutral (9-11 shade scale), Semantic (4 colors, 2-3 shades each), Surface (2-3 elevation levels).
Do: Use structured palette with clear roles

Description: This rule is about visual weight, not pixel count: 60% neutral backgrounds, 30% secondary colors, 10% accent.
Body text needs 4.5:1 (AA) or 7:1 (AAA). Large text (18px+ or 14px bold) needs 3:1 (AA) or 4.5:1 (AAA).
Light gray text on white, gray text on colored backgrounds, red on green, blue on red, yellow on white commonly fail contrast.
Don't: Use gray text on colored backgrounds

Description: Pure gray (oklch(50% 0 0)) and pure black (#000) don't exist in nature—real shadows and surfaces always have a color cast.
Don't: Use pure black (#000) or pure white (#fff)

Description: Dark mode is not inverted light mode. Dark mode requires different design decisions: lighter surfaces for depth (no shadows), reduce font weight slightly, desaturate accents.
Use two layers: primitive tokens (--blue-500) and semantic tokens (--color-primary: var(--blue-500)). For dark mode, only redefine the semantic layer.
Heavy use of transparency (rgba, hsla) usually means an incomplete palette. Alpha creates unpredictable contrast, performance overhead, and inconsistency.
